name: Papua New Guinea

colors.symbolism: a traditional colour throughout Papua New Guinea, often associated with celebration and ceremony
a traditional colour of Papua New Guinea, frequently used in tribal art and dress
the soaring Raggiana bird-of-paradise, and the wealth of the country
the five stars of the Southern Cross

description: Divided diagonally from upper hoist to lower fly: the upper-fly half is red and bears a yellow Raggiana bird-of-paradise in flight; the lower-hoist half is black and bears the five white stars of the Southern Cross.
history: Adopted on 1 July 1971, four years before Papua New Guinea gained independence from Australia on 16 September 1975. The design was the winning entry by 15-year-old schoolgirl Susan Karike in a national competition; her design was preferred over earlier proposals featuring the colours of the Australian-administered territory.

features.symbolism: the diagonal split from the upper-hoist to the lower-fly divides the flag into a red triangle at the fly and a black triangle at the hoist — the colours red and black are traditional throughout much of Papua New Guinea
the national bird of Papua New Guinea, depicted in flight to symbolise the emergence of the nation; its plumage and grace also represent the country's tribal heritage and natural beauty
the constellation Crux, marking Papua New Guinea's location in the Southern Hemisphere and historical ties with other countries of the South Pacific
